title: Offline Planning and Online Learning Under Recovering Rewards
abstract: Motivated by emerging applications, such as live-streaming e-commerce, promotions, and recommendations, we introduce and solve a general class of nonstationary multi-armed bandit problems that have the following two features: (i) the decision maker can pull and collect rewards from up toK(≥1)out ofNdifferent arms in each time period and (ii) the expected reward of an arm immediately drops after it is pulled and then nonparametrically recovers as the arm’s idle time increases. With the objective of maximizing the expected cumulative reward overTtime periods, we design a class of purely periodic policies that jointly set a period to pull each arm. For the proposed policies, we prove performance guarantees for both the offline and the online problems. For the offline problem when all model parameters are known, the proposed periodic policy obtains a long-run approximation ratio that is at the order of1−O(1/K), which is asymptotically optimal whenKgrows to infinity. For the online problem when the model parameters are unknown and need to be dynamically learned, we integrate the offline periodic policy with the upper confidence bound procedure to construct on online policy. The proposed online policy is proved to approximately haveO˜(NT)regret against the offline benchmark. Our framework and policy design may shed light on broader offline planning and online learning applications with nonstationary and recovering rewards.

article.1. Introduction: In the past decades, the study of sequential decision making has attracted prominent attention from academic researchers and industrial practitioners. Emerging applications, such as live-streaming e-commerce, promotions, and recommendations, have given rise to new features and posed challenges to classic sequential decision-making tools. In this paper, using the framework of multi-armed bandit (MAB) problems, we introduce and solve a general class of sequential decision-making problems that accommodate two relevant new features. The first feature is that, in each time period, the decision maker can simultaneously pull and collect rewards from at mostKarms out of a total ofNarms. WhenKis an arbitrary number that is greater than one, this feature generalizes the standard multi-armed bandit problem, accommodating applications in which the decision maker can pull more than one arm simultaneously in one time period. The second feature is that the expected reward of an arm immediately drops after it is pulled and then gradually recovers if the arm is not pulled in the subsequent time periods. This feature matches a number of phenomena in the area of management science, including one that customers may temporarily get bored if they are repeatedly recommended with the same product, same deal, or same type of promotion. The general class of multi-armed bandit problems with these two features may appear in broad applications that involve nonstationary and dynamically changing rewards. We use the example of live-streaming e-commerce to illustrate the problem setting and two new features in a simplified description. The rapidly growing live-streaming e-commerce is estimated to hit $100 billion in annual global sales in the year 2020 and is expected to exceed $300 billion in the year 2021; seeGreenwald (2020),Kharif and Townsend (2020), andEnberg (2021). A popular form of live-streaming e-commerce is session-based promotions held by a key opinion leader (KOL) on platforms such as Amazon Live, Taobao Live, and TikTok. In session-based promotions, a KOL holds sessions on consecutive days. For each session, the KOL chooses, say, aboutK= 50 products to discuss their features, promote, and sell during the session, one product after another, often at deep discounts. The prepared inventory for the promoted products can often sell out in seconds. Many manufacturers pitch top KOLs and create a large pool of products for a KOL to choose from with an estimated 10 to 1 selection ratio (Greenwald 2020,Kharif and Townsend 2020). Therefore, the entire pool of products can be of sizeN= 500. This emerging application of live-streaming e-commerce gives rise to the need for dynamic planning and learning in the presence of the two aforementioned features. First, in each time period, the decision maker needs to selectK= 50 products (arms) to promote and sell out of a pool ofN= 500. Second, if a product is promoted and on sale at some time periodtwith expected rewardR, the expected reward of promoting and selling the same product again in time periodt+ 1 drops and can be much smaller thanR. On the other hand, as the idle time (number of time periods in which a product has not been promoted) increases, the expected reward of the product can increase. See as an example section 4 ofShen et al. (2024)for empirical observations that may support the feature that the daily sales/revenue drop after a strong promotion day or period. Because of this feature, even for the most popular product, a KOL will not promote and sell the product in every single session, but alternatively, tend to select the product in every few other sessions. Aside from the example of live-streaming e-commerce, there are a number of other applications that exhibit such rewards dropping and recovering phenomenon. For example, in the recommendation domain, a customer may enjoy a particular product or service but may temporarily get bored immediately after consuming the product or service. In this case, the reward of recommending the same product or service to the customer temporarily drops after the most recent consumption but may gradually recover as time passes. Different products or services can have different dropping and recovering behavior, and it is in the decision maker’s interest to strategically choose when and how often to recommend each product or service. For any given product, a resting time between each time it is recommended can be valuable, and this intuitively offers a refreshing window for the customer. With the incorporation of the two new features, solving the proposed general class of sequential decision-making problems present new challenges and difficulties. The difficulties are centered around algorithm design and the establishment of theoretical guarantees. Specifically, even for the offline problem in which all model parameters are initially known but future realizations of uncertainties are unknown, it is computationally hard to obtain a near-optimal policy. First, in practice, the reward recovery mechanism typically may not exhibit nice structural patterns, such as concavity or parametric forms. In the presence of the potentially complicated reward recovery mechanism, it is challenging to obtain simple-to-implement policies that fit into a broad range of application settings with theoretical performance guarantees. Second, the reward for pulling an arm is concerned with not only the current time period, but also the time period when the arm was pulled last, which is essentially affected by the policy itself. Thus, unlike classic bandit problems, in this problem, there does not exist an optimal subset of arms to identify and eventually pull for each time period. The decision maker has to adjust the subset of pulled arms dynamically in a delicate way. For the online problem in which parameters need to be learned on the fly, we also face difficulties that are distinguishable from canonical online learning problems. First, apart from the classic exploration–exploitation dilemma, we also face a planning–learning trade-off. To improve the knowledge of the reward recovery mechanism, we need to plan for a delay to collect samples. However, planning for a long delay may cause a waste of time and insufficient learning. Second, the design of the offline optimization oracle in this online setting needs additional treatment. Typically, offline oracles are assumed to be powerful enough to solve given inputs with theoretical guarantees. However, because of the estimation errors caused by randomness, it is unclear whether the offline planning result can be directly adapted into the online learning one. In this paper, we overcome the difficulties mentioned. We summarize our contributions as follows. A general recovering model.To characterize the dropping and recovering behavior of the rewards, which is represented by a recovering function, we build a nonparametric bandit model with minimal assumptions on the recovery functions: monotonicity and boundedness. The recovery functions do not need to satisfy any other structural properties, such as concavity. Our model relaxes assumptions and generalizes settings in previous work, thus allowing wider potential applications. An offline planning framework.Even if the recovery functions are fully known, the planning problem of jointly optimally choosingKout ofNarms for each of theTtime periods is computationally difficult. We construct an upper bound on the offline planning problem, which leads us to focus on a simple and intuitive class of policies called purely periodic policies (PPP). Surprisingly, for the proposed policies, the long-run offline approximation ratio (the ratio between the expected cumulative reward of the constructed policy and the optimal) only depends onK(which we denote asγK) and does not depend on any structure of the recovery function. This ratio, moreover, has a uniform lower bound 1/2 and approaches 1 with aO(1/K)gap uniformly overK≥1. Further, we prove that thisO(1/K)gap is tight within the class of PPP up to a logarithmic factor, showing that any other PPP cannot do better than our algorithm asymptotically. The framework of our policy design and analysis is novel in the sense that we utilize power of two to bypass the difficulty of combinatorial planning and address the trade-off between rounding error and scheduling error. Moreover, our policy design framework does not rely on standard parametric and structural assumptions and can potentially be adapted to various other applications. A learning-while-planning algorithm.We address the problem in which we have no prior knowledge of the recovery functions and must learn through online samples. Enlightened by the offline planning part, we propose an online learning policy to solve the exploration–exploitation trade-off and, moreover, reach a balance between planning and learning. The policy incurs aO˜(T)regret compared with our offline result. Our design of the online learning algorithm exploits the sparse structure of the purely periodic policy proposed in our offline planning framework, relates it to a generalization of the knapsack problem, and integrates it with upper confidence bounds (UCBs), which may bring new insights to solve other online learning problems under recovering rewards. 1.1. Related Literature 1.1.1. Multi-Armed Bandits. The MAB problem has aroused great interest in both academia and industry in the last decade. Since the pioneer work ofAuer et al. (2002), researchers have developed many theoretically guaranteed algorithms based on the optimism under uncertainty principle. Readers could refer toLattimore and Szepesvári (2020)andSlivkins (2019)for a comprehensive discussion about this field. An important MAB variant is the combinatorial MAB problem, in which, in each time period, the player can pull a subset of arms instead of only one arm (see, e.g.,Kveton et al. 2015). This better captures real-world applications, and we also adopt the setting. Another variant of MAB arousing interest in recent years is nonstationary MAB, in which the mean reward of each arm can change with time. Two main nonstationary models are restless bandits (Whittle 1988) and rested bandits (Gittins 1979). In restless bandits, the mean reward of an arm changes irrespective of the policy being used, whereas in rested bandits, the mean reward changes only when the arm is pulled by the policy. These two problems have been investigated through many different perspectives, such as variation budget (Besbes et al. 2014), abrupt changes (Auer et al. 2019), rotting bandits (Levine et al. 2017), and applications in dynamic pricing (Keskin and Zeevi 2017,Zhu and Zheng 2020). We note that our setting is neither rested nor restless as our reward distribution of an arm changes according to both the time period itself and whether the arm is selected by the policy or not. Our problem exhibits a recovering behavior: the mean reward of an arm first drops after a pulling but gradually recovers as the idle time increases. Next, we review the bandit literature with recovering rewards, which is most related to our work. 1.1.2. Bandits with Recovering Rewards. Recently, there has been an increasing number of works studying bandits under different recovering environments. However, so far, all the work only considers pulling one arm at a time. The work most relevant to ours isKleinberg and Immorlica (2018). The authors develop a polynomial-time approximation scheme to obtain a long-run(1−ϵ)-optimal scheduling for the offline problem of which the time complexity is doubly exponential in1/ϵand derive aO˜(T/ϵO(1/ϵ))regret for the online problem by adapting the offline result. Their analysis is heavily dependent on the assumption that the recovery function is nondecreasing and globally concave. We remove this somewhat restrictive assumption by only assuming the monotonicity, allowing more general behavior after a pull. In this case, the property of the randomized policy discussed inKleinberg and Immorlica (2018)may no longer be valid, and thus, we propose different solution methods and new rounding techniques. A special case of our model is investigated inBasu et al. (2019), in which an arm cannot be pulled for a while after it is pulled. The “sleeping” time of each arm is known a priori. The authors compete their online learning algorithm with the greedy policy, which yields a long-run1−1/eapproximation guarantee. Because of the nice property of the greedy policy, some subsequent work follows this line and considers more general blocking bandit models (see, e.g.,Basu et al. 2021,Atsidakou et al. 2021). However, in our more general setting, the greedy policy may perform very badly compared with the true optimum. Also, in our online learning problem, we have no prior knowledge of the sleeping times.Cella and Cesa-Bianchi (2020)generalize the setting inBasu et al. (2019)using nonparametric recovery. However, they assume that the recovery rate is the same among all arms and only consider purely periodic policies in which all selected arms are pulled at the same frequency with no constant ratio guarantees. We adopt a more generalized model and take a broader approach by allowing different recovery rates and different pulling frequencies for different arms, which yields a 1/2 worst-case guarantee for the offline problem. Another related work isPike-Burke and Grunewalder (2019), in which the expected reward functions are sampled from a Gaussian process with a known kernel. The authors compare their algorithm to a Bayesiand-step lookahead benchmark, which is the greedy algorithm optimizing the nextdpulls given the decision maker’s current situation. In comparison, our benchmark is concerned with the total reward of the whole time horizonTrather than a prefixedd. Some other related work includesMintz et al. (2020)andYancey and Settles (2020). InMintz et al. (2020), the recovery function is characterized via a parametric form, whereas the authors obtain a worst-case regret ofO˜(T2/3).Yancey and Settles (2020)consider a specific application for the scheduling of reminder notifications. The authors demonstrate their policy via numerical experiments. We note that our problem is nonparametric in essence, and for the online problem, we have a theoretical guarantee ofO˜(T)regret compared with our offline benchmark. 1.1.3. Revenue Management with Reusable Resources. Our work is different from but related to revenue management problems with reusable resources. In the reusable resource setting, a resource can be used for some time by a user and returned to the system after the usage, and during the usage time, the resource is not available. Initialized byLevi and Radovanović (2010), there has been a large amount of work solving more complex management problems (matching and assortment optimization) with reusable resources (see, e.g.,Owen and Simchi-Levi 2018;Feng et al. 2019,2022;Goyal et al. 2020a,b;Dickerson et al. 2021;Rusmevichientong et al. 2021;Gong et al. 2022;Udwani 2022). In particular,Rusmevichientong et al. (2020)give a 1/2 approximation algorithm under (known) stochastic arrivals and blocking time distributions using approximate dynamic programming. They also show that their policy achieves a1−O(1/C1/3)approximation ratio as the minimum inventoryCgrows large.Gong et al. (2022)prove that the greedy policy has a 1/2 competitive ratio, using a novel queue-coupling technique to deal with the randomness incurred by the usage times.Talluri and Tsoukalas (2023)consider a revenue management problem of a professional services firm with quality revelation, which can be modeled under the reusable resource setting. They apply approximate dynamic programming to provide a series of bounds and solution methods. Whereas our work shares some similarities with previous research, there are several key differences. First, in the reusable resource setting, a resource’s reward is typically considered to be zero after its use and then recovers to its original value after a recharging period with each resource possessing only one intrinsic value. However, our setting allows for a more complex recovery mechanism, and in our setting, the decision maker has the flexibility to pull an arm at any time period. Second, most work on reusable resources focuses on applying approximate dynamic programming or fluid approximation to obtain policies that have good numerical performance or a provable theoretical guarantee. In this work, we focus on understanding a simple class of purely periodic policies that also offer straightforward interpretation and insights into the problem structure. Surprisingly, we find that this simple class of policies is enough to obtain asymptotically optimal performance in the recovering nonstationary setting. Finally, unlike much of the previous work, we consider the online learning counterpart of the offline planning problem and provide provable theoretical guarantees. It is uncertain whether methods such as approximate dynamic programming can produce provably efficient online learning policies. Our solution may be of independent interest to researchers working on revenue management with reusable resources.
article.2. Model and Assumptions: There areNarms in total. Denote[N]as the set{1,2,…,N}. LetKbe any positive integer. In each time periodt=1,2,3,…, the action is to choose a set of no more thanKarms from the totalNarms and simultaneously pull the chosen arms. The revenue collected in each time period is the sum of the reward of each pulled arm. The expected reward of an arm is not stationary and may change over time in the following way. For each armi, consider a set of nonnegative scalars{Ri(d)}d=0,1,2,…that construct the recovery functionRi(·)with respect tod. The reward of pulling armiat time periodtis a random variable with meanRi(t−t′), wheret′denotes the most recent time period (prior tot) when armiis pulled. We letRi(0)=0for completeness. For each armi, the randomness in the reward of pulling armiis assumed to be independent across time. We note that random variables of different arms may be dependent (see, e.g.,Chen et al. 2013). The system runs from time period 1 and continues in discrete time periods2,3,…. To clearly define the initialization of the system at time period 1, one needs to specify when is the last time period in which each arm is pulled before time period 1. There have been two common types of initialization. One is that the last time that each arm is pulled is−∞; seeBasu et al. (2019). The other is that the last time that each arm is pulled is time period 0, right before the start of the problem at time period 1; seeKleinberg and Immorlica (2018). We adopt the second type of initialization, and we note that there is no intrinsic difference between the first and second types regarding the analytical results to be established in this work. Next, we describe and discuss assumptions on the recovery function. (Monotonicity).For anyi∈[N], the recovery function{Ri(d)}d≥0is nondecreasing in d. The nondecreasing property of the expected reward sequence{Ri(d)}d≥0reflects the modeling feature that the longer the time for which an arm has been idle (not pulled), the larger the expected reward once the arm is pulled again. In fact, the range of reward models implied byAssumption 1includes that implied by the assumptions made in the literature—concave recharging bandits (Kleinberg and Immorlica 2018), sleeping/blocking bandits (Basu et al. 2019), and recovering bandits with identical recovery rate (Cella and Cesa-Bianchi 2020). Specifically, we extend the concave nondecreasing structure in the literature to the general nondecreasing structure, which assumes no specific function shape and allows more applications. (Boundedness).The reward of pulling an arm in each time period, as a random variable, is bounded by a known constantRmaxuniformly over alli∈[N]. The assumption of bounded random rewards is common in many applications, such as promotion in live-streaming e-commerce and recommendation. An alternate but slightly more restrictive assumption toAssumption 2for the mean rewards{Ri(d)}i∈[N],d≥0is called finite-time recovery. (Finite-Time Recovery).For anyi∈[N],∃dimax≥1such thatRi(d)=Ri(dimax)for alld≥dimax. Assumption 2′means that the mean reward of each arm recovers to a max nominal level and stays there after a finite number of idle time periods (see also, e.g.,Basu et al. 2019,Pike-Burke and Grunewalder 2019). We see that this assumption is added inSection 3primarily for computational concerns. All of our theoretical results and bounds do not depend on{dimax}that can be large in practice. Before diving deep into how to solve the model, we give some additional remarks on the model. In our model, we are assuming that the total expected collected reward is a linear sum of the expected reward collected by single items (Ri(d)), which is only affected by the idle time of the particular item. This may not hold in general (e.g., because of customer choice effects in recommendation) but can hold for certain real-world scenarios. For example, in live-streaming e-commerce, if different arms correspond to totally different types of products (e.g., food, household appliances, clothes, electronic products), then the promotion order or complementarity/substitution effects have little impact as people tend to consider different types of products independently when making purchasing decisions. Another example is in the case of machines that process tasks, in which the efficiency of a machine in each time period depends on how long it has been maintained since the last use. The reward of running a machine (the number of completed tasks by the machine) drops if it is used for consecutive time periods but recovers as the idle time or maintenance time increases. This setting can also be interpreted as assigning a number of workers to maximize the number of completed intense physical tasks, in which each person needs some time for rest after working. In these cases, we can assume that different arms or machines/people are independent, and there are no complex customer choice effects as in the live-streaming e-commerce setting.
article.3. The Offline Planning Problem: In this section, we describe the definition of a decision-making problem that we refer to as the offline planning problem. We discuss the challenges of solving this problem and propose a class of easy-to-implement offline policies that can alleviate these challenges. 3.1. Problem Statement The offline planning problem is given as follows. The knowledge of{Ri(d)}i∈[N],d≥0is fully known to the decision maker, and there is no unknown parameter. The randomness in the system does not impact the policy design for the offline problem because all the model parameters are known and there is no need to learn those parameters under uncertainties. The objective is to maximize the total expected reward throughout a time horizon ofTunder the constraint that no more thanKarms can be pulled in each time period. More precisely, we defineOPTR[K,T](sometimes abbreviated asOPT) as the maximal expected reward that can be obtained among all different policies: Here,Ji∈[T]is the number of times armiis pulled.si,1,…,si,Jiis the time schedule of pulling armi. The last group of constraints in (1) upper bounds the number of pulled arms in each time period byK. We note that exactly solving the offline planning problem (1) is computationally intractable and NP-hard. The problem setting inBasu et al. (2019)is a special case of ours, and they discuss in section 3 the computational intractability of their problem setting. Because our problem setting is more general and, in addition, allows arbitrary nonparametric recovery, the optimal solution can be even more complicated because of the nonparametric recovery. Our goal is to propose a framework of policies that enjoy two properties. First, the proposed policies have provable performances. Specifically, we hope to find an absolute constantγK>0possibly dependent onKand another constantβthat does not depend onTsuch that the total expected reward collected by the proposed policies is at least Second, the proposed policies should be easy to construct and implement. Mathematically, the policies should be run in polynomial time withK,N, andT. Moreover, the policies should be flexible enough to be adapted to more complex settings, such as the online learning problem in which the parameters are unknown a priori. A first thought is to consider the use of greedy policy, which is widely used and is shown to have reliable performances in many settings, including bandit problems with recovering rewards (Kleinberg and Immorlica 2018;Basu et al. 2019,2021). The greedy policy in our setting means that the policy selectsKarms with the highest expected reward in the current time period. However, it fails on the first point regarding the performance guarantee. In fact, the greedy policy may have arbitrarily bad expected total reward in terms of the ratio with the optimal as is shown inExample 1. LetN= 2 andK= 1. LetR1(d)=randR2(d)=𝟙d=1+R·𝟙d>1, whereR≫1>r. Then, under the greedy policy, we always pull arm 2, yielding a total reward ofT. However, if we pull arm 1 and 2 in turn, then the total reward is lower bounded byR+r2(T−1)≫TwhenR≫1. Therefore, the ratio between the expected total reward achieved by the greedy policy and the optimal can be arbitrarily small. Note that, as a comparison, in the problem settings ofKleinberg and Immorlica (2018)andBasu et al. (2019), the greedy policy in an analogous form, despite being suboptimal, has a guaranteed lower bound at least 1/2 in terms of the ratio between the expected total reward achieved by the greedy policy and the optimal. This also suggests that our problem setting that allows arbitrary nonparametric recovery has more subtleties. 3.2. Purely Periodic Policy (PPP) We have seen that a naive greedy policy may not perform well for generalRin the worst case. In the following, we alternatively propose the use of a simple and easy-to-implement class of policies called purely periodic policies. (Purely Periodic Policy). A policy is called purely periodic if, for eachi∈[N], the policy assignsti∈Zanddi∈Z+∪{+∞}such that (i)ti∈(−di,0]and (ii) armiis pulled at timeti+k·di(k∈Z+)until the time horizonTis exhausted. A purely periodic policy is represented by{(di,ti)}i∈[N]. We note that, for a purely periodic policy, whendi=+∞,ti≤0for somei, the policy never pulls armithroughout the whole time horizon. (K-PPP). LetK∈Z+. A policy is called aK-PPP if the policy is a purely periodic policy{(di,ti)}i∈[N]and, meanwhile, in each time periodt≥1, at mostKarms are pulled at the same time. Equivalently speaking, With an overall number ofNarms, each PPP is always anN-PPP. For general (K,N), the set ofK-PPPs is a subset within the class of PPPs. Despite its simplicity, the family of PPPs indeed owns nice properties. On the computational side, it can be constructed in polynomial time. On the theoretical side, the long-run approximation ratioγKis lower bounded by an absolute constant and approaches one asKincreases. It turns out that these properties are quite nontrivial, and we devote the next section to show how. We note that finding the long-run optimal policy for the offline problem is NP-hard even if we confine ourselves within the class ofK-PPP. In the online appendix, we give a proof demonstrating this point. There are three main challenges for constructing aK-PPP that delivers superior performance. First, we need to determine the set of arms that are included in the planning schedule:{i:di<+∞}. Second, we need to find appropriate frequencies{1/di}for selected arms or, equivalently, the set of periods{di}. Finally, we need to pair eachdiwith sometias defined inDefinition 2. The{di}’s must be selected carefully, or there may not exist such{ti}that no more thanKarms can be pulled at the same time. We illustrate this point through the following example. Letn= 2 andK= 1. Then, there exists no 1-PPP such thatd1=2andd2=3because two and three are coprime numbers. No matter how we chooset1andt2, there must exist a time period such that both arms are scheduled to be pulled together, violating the constraintK= 1. However, ifd1=2andd2=4, then lettingt1=−1andt2=−2yields a feasible 1-PPP.

We first discuss how to construct a reasonable upper bound and its implications on PPP. Then, we propose a rounding-scheduling (R-S) framework about constructing aK-PPP. Finally, we present (long-run) approximation guarantees for our proposed algorithm as well as its tightness. 4.1. Bounding the Objective: Why Purely Periodic? The goal of this part is to define a reasonable baseline for our problem. Specifically, we give an upper bound on the optimal (expected) objective value of the problem (OPT) when the rewards recovery functions are fully known. UnderAssumption 2′, an optimal solution of the concave program (3) can be efficiently computed because (3) can be rewritten as a linear program.Kleinberg and Immorlica (2018)consider the case in whichK= 1 anddi(k)=k(∀k≥0)and suggest that (3) admits a fully polynomial-time approximation scheme (FPTAS), which takes as input an instance of the problem and a parameterϵ>0and outputs a value that is at least1−ϵtimes the optimal value with runtime polynomial in the problem size and in1/ϵeven ifAssumption 2′is violated. article.5. The Online Learning Problem: In this section, we turn to address the online counterpart of the offline planning problem. In the online problem, unlike inSection 3, the recovery function{Ri(d)}i,dis not known a priori and should be learned from the sequential samples for anyi. Our goal is to construct an online learning policy that achieves small regret compared with the offline result inSection 3. To be precise, we want to design an online learning algorithm such that theγK-regret, defined by the difference betweenUBR[K]and the expected total reward obtained by running the algorithm through a time horizon ofT, is sublinear inTfor generalRsatisfyingAssumptions 1and2. Furthermore, we hope that the algorithm is adaptive in the sense that it can obtain better performance guarantee when additional assumptions are made onR, particularly if the offline planning problem admits a (long-run) approximation ratio better thanγK. Design Framework Broadly speaking, our policy design is built upon the optimism under uncertainty principle that is successfully applied in many online learning problems. article.7. Synthetic Experiments: In this section, we use synthetic experiments to demonstrate the performance of our algorithms (proposed and analyzed inSections 4–6). We note that, for the planning problem with a very general recovering reward model and the allowance to pull multiple arms at the same time, existing policies (see, e.g.,Kleinberg and Immorlica 2018,Papadigenopoulos and Caramanis 2021) do not have direct generalizations to such a planning problem setting except for the greedy policy (see, e.g.,Basu et al. 2019,Atsidakou et al. 2021). Further, existing policies do not have direct generalizations that address the online learning version of our general setting even for the greedy policy. Therefore, our plan in this section is to demonstrate our proved theoretical guarantees and gain insights on how the performance of our algorithms depend on different parameters. In the offline planning problem, we show through experiments the dependence on the problem parameterK. article.8. Conclusion: In this work, we consider the problem of dynamic planning and learning under a general bandit model of nonstationary recovering rewards. The nonstationarity stems from both the time elapsed and the policy itself. Solving the offline problem in which all recovery functions are known is computationally hard, so we focus on a simple class of purely periodic policies. We develop a new framework for rounding and scheduling to obtain a policy with provable performance guarantee. The long-run approximation ratio is shown to be uniformly lower bounded by 1/2 and asymptotically optimal with respect to the number of arms allowed to be pulled. We then show how to solve the online learning problem withO˜(T)regret compared with the offline benchmark. We design our algorithm through a novel combination of our offline result, the knapsack problem, and upper confidence bounds, bypassing the difficulties of planning while learning under recovering rewards. There is some relevant future work in line. For the offline planning problem, it is intriguing to see if we can build other classes of policies other than the class of PPP such that the approximation can be improved. In the online appendix, we show that, by combining a tweaked version of the greedy policy and a PPP, we can obtain a (long-run) approximation of 0.546, breaking the barrier of 1/2. It remains a question of what is the optimal approximation ratio we can achieve using polynomial-time algorithms. Also, in the example of live-streaming e-commerce, the customer choice effect can play an important role, and the linear structure may be twisted because of factors such as promotion order or complementarity/substitution effects. However, we note that modeling this phenomenon under the recovering structure can be challenging and may make the model too complex to compute and apply. One possible direction is to consider the assortment optimization problem and start from the basic multinomial logit model or to incorporate interactive terms for each pair of arms into the reward. Even with an established model, it is unclear whether we can obtain a meaningful upper bound (UBR[K]in our case) in the offline planning problem similar toProgram (3)in our case. When the customer effect is considered, the total reward collected is no longer separable by arms, and it may be difficult to construct a benchmark value for comparison in numerical experiments. Nevertheless, we believe that exploring this topic may be a very interesting future direction. In the online learning problem, it may be worth investigating whether the regret bound can be improved toO˜(NKT). Particularly, in the online appendix, we point out that the regret bound may be further improved if additional structures are added among different arms. Also, although our online learning regret bounds hold even when correlated rewards are allowed, better bounds may be achievable. We believe our work opens up new insights for future work to solve broader planning and learning problems with nonstationary and recovering rewards.
